article_meta.title: By Jonah Goldberg July 11, 2016 11:14 AM PT Share via Close extra sharing options Email Facebook X LinkedIn Threads Redd

reasoning: This is an opinion column by Jonah Goldberg, which complicates the analysis since opinion columns are expected to contain the author's views. The model identifies several instances of author-level bias in the author's own prose, with no attribution rule violations (no quoted text is flagged as author bias). The flagged items are defensible as bias patterns in the author's prose. However, there are some nuances: this is explicitly an opinion column, so 'Opinion Statements Presented as Fact' is a somewhat questionable label since the entire piece is opinion. The model could have also flagged items like 'knocked the lazy certainty out of almost everybody' or 'Rudy Giuliani (who did not lose his lazy certainty)' as Word Choice/Mudslinging, or the characterization of liberals as having 'seemingly boundless faith' as a straw man. Overall, the model performs reasonably well - correct attribution compliance, defensible flagged items, and mostly appropriate type labels.

custom_reasoning.attribution_rule_compliance: All four flagged items are from the author's own prose. No quoted text or attributed source language is flagged. The Fryer quote and other attributed statements are correctly excluded.
custom_reasoning.false_positive_rate: Most flagged items are defensible. The 'ghoulish/dark cloud' flagging is reasonable as author emotionalism. The 'national conversations' item is debatable since this is an opinion column. The 'obtuse' and 'boundless faith' items are genuine loaded language from the author. However, flagging opinion as bias in an explicitly labeled opinion column is somewhat questionable - though the instructions don't exempt opinion pieces.
custom_reasoning.false_negative_rate: The model misses some notable instances: 'knocked the lazy certainty out of almost everybody' (Word Choice), the parenthetical dig at Giuliani '(who did not lose his lazy certainty)' (Mudslinging/Ad Hominem or Word Choice), 'vilifying those sworn to uphold it' (Word Choice/Spin), and potentially the repeated rhetorical framing of 'Conservatives, of all people' as a rhetorical device. These are moderate omissions.
custom_reasoning.bias_type_accuracy: The labels are mostly defensible but not perfectly applied. 'Opinion Statements Presented as Fact' for a statement in an opinion column is debatable. 'Spin' for 'obtuse' could also be 'Word Choice' or 'Mudslinging/Ad Hominem.' 'Slant' for the liberal characterization is reasonable. 'Subjective Qualifying Adjectives' for the opening metaphor could also be 'Sensationalism/Emotionalism' or 'Word Choice.' Labels are in the right ballpark but not always the most precise choice.
